data.title: Cryptocurrencies Are Loving The Rally In Bitcoin
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Cryptocurrencies have yo-yo'd quite aggressively in the past two months, moving from the dovish FED narrative to widespread profit-taking after failing to pursue the 2025 bull-move.

Some concerns had been raised, and with justification.

The Crypto total Market Cap had been free falling, some key ETFs had been seeing their first yearly outflows and technicals were corroborating a double top in both Ethereum and Bitcoin, adding to the shaded picture.

But amid the ceaseless everything rally, buyers stepped up to create a lower high in Bitcoin as Equity indices were getting lifted and even more as the US government shutdown put back some diversification-interest in investors' plans.

The Rally in BTC followed up with a huge day for altcoins yesterday.

As a matter of fact, no NFP releases in today's data may add to the ongoing easy-going flows – For now the crypto picture is muted but October marked a very positive sign for Crypto Market.

After looking at the current daily performance, we'll have a look at ETH, SOL and XRP through some intraday charts.Update: Russia just lifted the ban for Crypto investing on their main stock exchange. Amid the ongoing war and frozen asset purchases around the world for Russians, this may attract further flows to the digital assets.

Daily overview of the Crypto Market (10:41 ET), October 3, 2025 – Source: Finviz

A picture that was mostly red at the top of the hour is now turning green, with BNB seeing some strong inflows. Before that, the mood was more of mild profit-taking – Some Risk-on flows post Services PMIs are now spreading into digital assets.

Watch afternoon flows, as recently, we've seen some huge movements particularly around the 14:30 mark (take a look at the change in crypto picture from the Sep 29 session).

Market Cap checkup – Back towards recent highs

Total Crypto Market Cap, October 3, 2025 – Source: TradingView

The signs of profit-taking from mid-September have now extinguished, with the Market Cap flashing below the December 2024 record ($3.76T).

A move below could have started to trigger some fears for participants who are leveraged at the highs, but the market is for now holding strong.

Let's now look at some charts.

Intraday Charts for ETH, SOL and XRP

Ethereum (ETH) 8H Chart

Ethereum (ETH) 8H Chart, October 3, 2025 – Source: TradingView

Ethereum has maintained strong momentum since the end of September but is a bit left off in today's action.

Unchanged with a slowing RSI, the picture is still looking better than it did, particularly with prices moving above the $4,200 to $4,300 consolidation, which acted as key pivot for bull/bear strength.

Keep an eye on the downward trendline connecting all tops since the new record highs from August as it seems to be containing the action.

Any break above should lead to a retest of the ATH.

Levels to place on your ETH Charts:

Support Levels:

  • $4,200 to $4,300 consolidation Zone
  • $4,000 to $4,095 Main Long-run Pivot
  • $3,900 8H MA 200
  • $3,500 Main Support Zone

Resistance Levels:

  • $4,600 mini-resistance August 26th peak
  • $4,950 Current new All-time highs
  • $4,700 to $4,950 All-time high resistance zone
  • Potential main resistance $5,230 Fibonacci extension

Solana (SOL) 8H Chart

Solana (SOL) 8H Chart, October 3, 2025 – Source: TradingView

Now up 20% from its recent trough at $190.60, Solana has re-attracted significant inflows.

Beating its competitor the Ether in the past week, Solana still has to watch for reactions at the confluence of the $235 mini-resistance and the top of the upward channel.

Breaking above should test the previous $253 highs, after that, nothing much to stop the reaching of new all-time highs.

A rejection here however could point to a retest of the $200 level.

Watch for an also high RSI that may need more momentum to counteract.

Levels to keep on your SOL Charts:

Support Levels:

  • Resistance turned pivot level $218 to $220
  • Support zone $200 to $205
  • Recent lows $191
  • $185 higher timeframe momentum support

Resistance Levels:

  • $235 to $240 mini-resistance and Higher bound of channel
  • $250 to $255 main resistance
  • $290 to $300 all-time high resistance ($295 ATH)

XRP 8H Chart

XRP 8H Chart, October 3, 2025 – Source: TradingView

XRP has officially broken out of its descending channel that had compromised its outlook, and having retested it, the picture is brighter.

Overall, the technicals for Ripple are more rangebound than anything, with prices holding between $2.65 to $3.15 since mid-August, but reactions here could be key.

Momentum has been strong, but buyers will need to breach the mini-resistance between $3.10 to $3.20 to try to regain the $3.40 highs, levels not seen since end-July/beginning-August.

This would also breach the current range formation.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Antero Midstream: Colorado Supreme Court Takes Up The Veolia Lawsuit Case
data.description:
Antero Midstream's (NYSE:AM) general business outlook looks good, with a solid longer-term outlook for continued volume growth. However, there has been some negative news around the Veolia lawsuit situation, with Veolia's petition for certiorari being granted by the Colorado Supreme Court.

The Colorado Supreme Court only granted 6% of petitions for certiorari in its last fiscal year, and this now results in a significant possibility that the lower court decision could be reversed.

As a result, I have reduced my estimate of Antero Midstream's value from $20 in my August article to $19.70 per share. I had previously assumed it was very likely that Antero Midstream would receive roughly $300 million in lawsuit proceeds soon, but have now adjusted its estimated value to account for the increased uncertainty.

I am moving from a "Buy" rating to a "Neutral" outlook on Antero Midstream in the near-term, as its current share price is fairly close to that estimated value. I believe that Antero Midstream's longer-term outlook remains solid, but there is potential for a modest negative impact in 2026 if the Colorado Supreme Court reverses the lower court damages award.

Veolia Lawsuit Update

In early September 2025, Veolia's petition for certiorari was granted by the Colorado Supreme Court regarding the issue of "whether the economic loss rule bars a fraud claim when the parties were in a contractual relationship."

If the petition for certiorari was denied, then the case would be over and Antero Midstream would be set to receive approximately $300 million. I had assumed this would very likely be the result since the Colorado Supreme Court only granted 6% of the petitions for certiorari in the fiscal year ending June 2025.

This result is a negative for Antero Midstream, as it opens up a significant possibility that the damages award gets overturned or reduced. As well, even if the lower court ruling is affirmed in the end, this probably delays the end of the case until mid-2026.

Debt Refinancing

Antero Midstream recently issued $650 million in 5.75% unsecured notes due 2033 at par. It expected to receive $642 million in net proceeds after expenses.

These funds (plus a small amount of credit facility borrowings) are being used to redeem Antero's $650 million in 5.75% unsecured notes due 2027. These notes are being redeemed at 100% of principal plus accrued but unpaid interest.

The weighted average interest rate of Antero Midstream's remaining notes is 5.76%, and its credit facility had an interest rate of 5.91% at the end of Q2 2025.

Thus in the current environment, Antero Midstream's future debt refinancing efforts likely won't affect its interest costs much.

Antero can reduce its interest costs somewhat through paying down its credit facility debt. If it fully pays off its credit facility debt in the future, that would boost its annual free cash flow by around $0.05 per share.

Notes On Valuation

I had previously estimated Antero Midstream's value at $20 per share, but am now trimming that to approximately $19.70 per share.

This reduction reflects the increased uncertainty around the Veolia lawsuit, now that the Colorado Supreme Court has granted Veolia's petition for certiorari.

Granting the petition doesn't automatically mean that the Colorado Supreme Court will overturn the lower court decision, but there is still a significant chance of that happening for any case they are willing to hear.

I haven't come across stats specific to the Colorado Supreme Court, but the U.S. Supreme Court has typically reversed (at least in part) 70+% of lower court rulings.

Antero Midstream's financial position is still quite strong even if it doesn't receive the $300 million in lawsuit proceeds (or if the damages award is reduced). However, that additional money would be beneficial for accelerating Antero Midstream's debt reduction and/or share repurchases.

Conclusion

Antero Midstream received negative news about the Veolia lawsuit, with the Colorado Supreme Court granting Veolia's petition for certiorari regarding one issue. The Colorado Supreme Court only granted 6% of the petitions for certiorari last fiscal year.

This delays Antero Midstream's potential receipt of lawsuit proceeds into mid-2026 and also results in a significant chance of the damages award being reversed or reduced.

As a result, I have reduced my estimate of Antero Midstream's value from $20 to $19.70 per share. Antero Midstream's longer-term volume growth potential remains solid, but it may not receive the $300 million near-term cash boost that I was previously expecting.

data.title: ISM Services PMI: Activity Unchanged In September
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Institute for Supply Management ('ISM') released its September Services Purchasing Managers' Index ('PMI'), with the headline composite index at 50.0. This was lower than the forecast of 51.8 and puts the index at the breakeven point between expansion and contraction for the first time since January 2010.

Here is an excerpt from the report summary:

Miller continues, “September’s Services PMI® level returned to numbers very similar to May and July, with weakness in business activity and continued weakness in employment. The recovery in the Backlog of Orders Index to a reading indicating slower contraction was a positive signal, as was continued expansion in the New Orders index. Commentary in general indicated moderate or weak growth, with more isolated observations of supplier delivery challenges. Employment continues to be in contraction territory, thanks to a combination of delayed hiring efforts and difficulty finding qualified staff.”

Here is a table showing the PMI's components.

Unlike its much older kin, the ISM manufacturing series, there is relatively little history for ISM's non-manufacturing data, especially for the headline composite Index, which dates from 2008. The chart below shows the non-manufacturing composite.

The more interesting and useful sub-component is the non-manufacturing business activity Index. The latest data point for September is 49.9, the lowest level since June 2024.

For a diffusion index, this can be an extremely volatile indicator, hence the addition of a six-month moving average to help us visualize the short-term trends.

Theoretically, this indicator should become more useful as the time frame of its coverage expands. Manufacturing may be a more sensitive barometer than non-manufacturing activity, but we are increasingly a services-oriented economy, which explains our intention to keep this series on the radar.

ISM PMI Comparison: Manufacturing vs. Services

The next chart offers a comparison between the two Purchasing Managers' Indexes provided by the Institute for Supply Management. See the latest update on the Manufacturing PMI here.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TSMC Q3 Preview: A Dividend Compounder Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
After delivering six straight quarters of double-digit revenue growth that finally stood at 54 percent in the June 2025 quarter, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Ltd. (NYSE:TSM) aka TSMC is trading near its all-time-high of $296.72. All eyes are on October 16, when TSMC reports its third fiscal quarter results after the market closes.

I will, of course, touch on the company's - and analysts' - performance expectations for the upcoming earnings release, but I shall also weave in an interesting dividend growth thesis that makes TSMC's price journey look that much more attractive.

Why Should One Hold This Stock?

Like it or not, every investor has to answer this crucial question of themself when holding an asset long-term. More often than not, one finds oneself invested in something because the run one had thus far was fantastic. No fundamental argument can shake that position because it is so entrenched. I have had Nvidia (NVDA) bulls disparage my profession and my qualifications, and one gentleman even referred to me, Elizabeth, as a person of the male persuasion! All because they disagreed with what I said.

This was the moment I realized that all things considered, the story we tell ourselves is the one that we believe the most. Any other view, even a tangentially opposing one, is such an affront to our sensibilities that we are willing to pen a verbal take-down - with straw in place of substance - and insult another human being just because of a difference of opinion? Have we come to that?

The trouble with this sort of view is that it is self defeating, and it blinds you to important signs along your investment journey. TSM is a perfect example of that. The negative buzz around country risk has been a terribly strong headwind to the stock. I could hardly believe it when I saw TSM still trading at these levels below.

GAAP earnings tell of undervaluation, but I would like to guide your gaze to the forward PEG ratio because I think this is a highly misunderstood metric. It is a forward-looking metric, of course, but it is also the market's expectations in a nutshell. The sector median tells us where the 'average' companies in that sector lie with respect to this metric, but far more important in my view is how this is interpreted.

TSM trades at a 25 percent discount to projected earnings growth over the next three to five years, based on analysts' growth projections for that period, which is about 21.6 percent. Now, that estimated growth rate is, by itself, more than 45 percent higher than the sector median for "EPS FWD Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR)."

Why does that not make sense? It would seem that the Street is expecting a much higher growth rate from TSM, but the market is rewarding that in a very muted fashion, with P/E almost equidistant from its median, but to the downside.

The only reason it can be this low is that the market is still very much hesitant to award it an appropriate earnings multiple because of the country risk and all this confusion around tariffs, moving production overseas, and how that balance can be successfully achieved. The trailing GAAP PEG ratio is even more abysmal, and this should clue us in that there is value waiting to be unlocked.

Over and above all this, the market does not seem to appreciate TSM from a dividend growth perspective, either.

The Secret of Dividend Growth

This is another underappreciated quality of a mission-critical company like TSM. The yield is not an impressive one at a shade over 1 percent, but when you look at the dividend growth metrics, it is immediately apparent that this is not priced into the stock, either. Per Seeking Alpha's data, TSM has been paying a dividend for two decades, and over the past three years the dividend has grown at an annualized rate of over 15 percent. Can it afford to keep up that level of dividend growth? We shall see.

The payout ratio is about 29 percent for Q2 2025, the last reported quarter. Despite TTM revenue growth of nearly 40 percent and a net margin of over 42 percent, this impressive dividend growth rate is putting just the right amount of pressure on free cash flow. For context, FCF for the TTM period is approximately $32 billion ($73 billion in operating cash flow, less CapEx of $41 billion, per my calculation), and the dividends paid over that period totaled about $14.2 billion, or a payout ratio of about 44 percent.

The only reason this looks high is that TSM's CapEx was around $41 billion against an operating cash flow of $73 billion. Net income for the TTM period was about $49.4 billion. Regardless, a sub-45 percent dividend payout ratio is well within reasonable bounds for a capital-intensive company.

What is truly attractive about dividend growth companies is that one's yield on cost will keep increasing as the stock moves higher over time.

In simple terms, if you had bought TSM back in 2013 or prior to that, your dividend yield will now be in double digits based on your much lower cost basis. This is where compounding shines through like a diamond, but one must be patient to reap this kind of reward.

Therefore, I put forward my case that TSM is an excellent long-term stock pick for any dividend growth portfolio.

And what should one make of its income statement dynamics in recent quarters and years? After all, in order to sustain this level of dividend growth the company should be growing its earnings at or above that rate. Is it? We shall see.

Performance and Financials are Impeccable

TSM has maintained top line growth in double-digit percentages for six straight quarters now, and it is literally crushing the Rule of 40, traditionally a SaaS metric. If we apply the Rule of 40, we need to look at topline and EBITDA growth, unlike the use of FCF for a SaaS company. Viewed through that lens, TSM attains Rule of 40 scores that are unheard of with a manufacturing company. Quite literally off the charts!

This is a capital-intensive manufacturing company that is performing like it is a SaaS company. One should be impressed by these figures because it is a rarity in the manufacturing world for a company to be reporting this kind of EBITDA growth. This also validates the key position of TSM in the global semiconductor supply chain. As we migrate to smaller nodes, the kind of expertise required to achieve profitable production yields increases exponentially, and TSM has that expertise like nobody else does.

From a performance and growth standpoint, it is hard to fault TSM on any metric. When we move to the balance sheet, we see an equally healthy trend on the books.

Shareholder equity has been growing by leaps and bounds on the back of earnings surplus (retained earnings) growth in strong double-digit percentages. The company also sports some very attractive liquidity ratios with its $90 billion in cash and short-terms against total liabilities of $81.8 billion. For all practical purposes, TSM can fulfil every single current and long-term obligation with just the cash and securities on hand. Liquid, and tremendously so.

Where Are We Headed with Q3 and Beyond?

At the Q2 conference call, management provided Q3 revenue guidance in the range of "between USD31.8 billion and USD33 billion, which represents an 8% sequential increase, or a 38% year-over-year increase at the midpoint."

Compared to the Q2 growth rate of 54 percent year over year, that seems like a significant drop, but it is in line with how TSM ended the final quarter of the prior fiscal year, FY 2024, when it achieved a 36 percent growth rate.

One particular metric that bears watching is the gross margin. With fabs now shifting to outside markets such as the U.S. (Arizona) and Japan (Kumamoto), management has already warned of margin pressure from elevated manufacturing costs at these newer fabs. The situation may ease some investors' minds because it spreads and therefore reduces the country risk we spoke about. The trade-off is lower gross margins for the foreseeable future, but management has been quite transparent with this:

We continue to forecast the gross margin dilution from the ramp-up of our overseas fabs in the next five years, starting from 2025, to be between 2% to 3% every year in the early stages and widen to 3% to 4% in the latter stages.

Average gross margin for the past five years is about 55 percent, but there will be additional pressure until the company can optimize costs at the more expensive Arizona fab. We are likely looking at something in the mid 40s long-term, in my view. I am confident that TSM can rein in the costs over time, as is the plan, but for now that is the most likely explanation for the low PEG ratio; earnings may not grow as expected due to the gross margin pressure in upcoming quarters.

Of course, that 55 percent could be a bit misleading because more recent quarters have seen GMs in the high 50s. I see mid-40s as the absolute worst case scenario - barring what I can only term a political force majeure.

On the news front, TSM is firm on its position on not capitulating on a reported 50-50 production split between the U.S. and other foundries. As reported by Seeking Alpha earlier this week:

"I want to clarify that this is the US's idea. Our negotiation team has never made a 50-50 commitment to a chip split," the island's top trade negotiator, Vice Premier Cheng Li-chiun told reporters in Taipei.

"Please be rest assured that we did not discuss this issue this time, and we will not agree to such a condition," she added.

My view on this is that production at the Arizona plant may be the lesser of two devils that TSM may well have to contend with. Taiwan is still in the midst of negotiations with the U.S., and a 20 percent tariff is still on the table. I do like the fact that TSM is being transparent about the potential hit to GMs through to 2030, but this is a wrinkle that will, in my view, be eventually ironed out over a long holding period.

My Takeaways

What I took away from this careful look at one of the world's most critical industry lynchpins - and the leading edge of the chip race - is that this would make an excellent dividend growth stock, and I doubt that the majority of investors look at it this way. The few who do will agree with my view. And why not? One of the most in-demand product lines the world has ever seen, the biggest moat a company could have, superior margins that are not imminently under threat, and a growing dividend that's already raking in oodles of cash for its early investors.

I have never believed that one can miss the bus on truly value-rich investments. I know some of you might bring up my bearish views on Nvidia as a negation of my thesis for TSM, but these are two very different companies whose only material commonality is that they both serve many of the same mega-cap tech behemoths. Nvidia is far more dependent on TSM than the other way around. For TSM, it would be a simple matter of finding replacement revenues, albeit it is an admittedly large chunk; for Nvidia, the result would be disastrous because it is on the supply side, with few to no alternatives for replacement.

That Taiwan is under so much political pressure because of this one company (among others, of course) should be a clear sign to anyone that vested parties will only go so far to try and claim it as belonging to them alone. The U.S. may be trying to onshore about half its production but TSM will have none of that, and the country is behind it because every unit of production moved overseas means fewer jobs for the native population.

As I leave you with these thoughts, I shall end by saying that TSM is the dividend growth play you probably did not know existed. This company is likely to be around for a very long time, and the emergence of AI has all but put a seal on that. If you get in now, when valuation has yet to catch up with value, your payout could eventually grow to represent a substantial portion of your cash flows. Buying ahead of the Q3 earnings release could potentially mean an immediate gain if the numbers come in as expected; worst case, the market gets spooked by gross margin impact and the stock slides, and you can enlarge your position at an even lower cost basis. A Taiwan-win situation!

TSM is an unequivocal Strong Buy for me.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ServiceTitan: Solid Drivers In Place To Grow For The Foreseeable Future
data.description:
Summary

I give a buy rating to ServiceTitan (NASDAQ:TTAN). In my view, the Roto-Rooter win demonstrates that TTAN can handle enterprise-scale complexity, while net dollar retention above 110% shows customers are embedding the platform more deeply. Layering on “Atlas,” which shifts TTAN from reactive workflows to proactive automation, gives me confidence that TTAN can sustain >20% growth and re-rate higher over time.

Company overview

TTAN provides a vertical, cloud-based software platform designed to be the end-to-end operating system for the trades industry. This platform connects and manages business workflows for contractors, including customer relationship management [CRM], field service management [FSM], enterprise resource planning [ERP], human capital management [HCM], and financial technology [FinTech]. TTAN serves a variety of trades that work on residential homes, commercial buildings, and new construction projects, such as HVAC, plumbing, electrical, roofing, and other exterior services.

Enterprise Momentum Validated by Landmark Customer Win

A key aspect of my bullish view for TTAN is that the success rate of the upmarket strategy has gone up a notch following its recent partnership with Roto-Rooter, the largest plumbing and drain cleaning provider in North America. In my opinion, this is not just another logo win but is a clear validation that TTAN’s platform is enterprise-ready and capable of handling scale and complexity at the very top end of the market. If you look at the profile of Roto-Rooter, it is a sophisticated, multi-location, multi-trade operator, which I expect to form the sizeable portion of the industry in the coming decades as these operators scale larger, which gives them more cost and reach advantage vs. smaller players (i.e., large operators to win more share). The fact that such a prominent brand chose to use TTAN really underscores how solid TTAN’s platform is, and if TTAN can serve Roto-Rooter, it should be able to serve all other customers with similar complexity.

From an investor's point of view, this deal de-risks the growth narrative. It shows that TTAN’s multi-year investments in commercial workflows and enterprise-grade functionality are translating into real customer wins. Financially, while the revenue contribution won’t fully ramp until early 2026, it creates a visible tailwind that should support a >20% subscription growth profile well in the coming years.

Deeply Entrenched Platform

Drilling into customer dynamics, I think data provided at the recent Pantheon conference that shows how valuable TTAN is to customers is the net dollar retention rate [NDRR], which once again came in >110% for the recent quarter. In my view, this is literally the best evidence of a platform seeing increasing adoption, and in the case of TTAN, making it stickier (because customers adopt more solutions).

Think of it this way: for every $100 in revenue TTAN had from a group of customers last year, that same group is now paying them over $110 this year. This isn't just about retaining customers; it's about growing them organically—customers are buying more seats for their employees, upgrading to premium tiers with more functionality, or cross-selling into new product modules. The underlying assumption is that customers must have seen so much value in TTAN’s product that they are actively choosing to embed TTAN's solutions even more deeply into their daily workflows.

This dynamic is precisely what creates a stickier platform and raises switching costs. As a customer adopts more of TTAN's modules, the platform transforms from a simple tool into their central operating system. As such, the cost and operational disruption required to rip out such a deeply integrated system and retrain staff on a new one become prohibitively high.

What makes this dynamic even better is the ongoing wave of private equity consolidation in the trades (per the 10Q filing and the investor day presentation). This is a lucrative dynamic for TTAN because PE-backed customers show faster GTV growth and higher adoption rates for value-added Pro modules. I think it makes sense because these private equity investors are focused on optimizing an unoptimized business before reselling it, and the best way to do it is to adopt software to achieve the ideal operational efficiency. In this case, I believe TTAN is a prime target. You can then see PE investors as a growth driver for GTV. As they continue to roll up businesses, those that have used TTAN will continue to use TTAN.

AI Strategy Positions ServiceTitan as the System of Action

While many software firms are layering on AI features, I think TTAN’s launch of “Atlas” strikes me as something far more important. This isn’t just another co-pilot added onto a workflow; I believe it structurally shifts the platform to become a proactive, autonomous “system of action” for the trades.

Essentially, instead of just reacting to user commands like a typical assistant, a "system of action" proactively and autonomously runs parts of the business on its own. Think of it as the difference between an assistant who can answer your questions and a skilled manager who identifies opportunities and acts on them without being told. For instance, Atlas can see that a technician's schedule is light and automatically create and launch a marketing campaign to fill the empty slots. It can also recognize when a technician finishes a job early and is near another customer with an appointment in two days, then autonomously text that customer to offer the earlier time slot, creating new capacity out of thin air. In short, “Atlas” connects all the different parts of the platform to automate entire functions, sometimes with zero human intervention, just like a human manager would.

Here is the thing: anyone can create such an AI feature, but not everyone can deliver the same results. Hence, the real moat here is TTAN’s data. Because it serves as the end-to-end system of record, from marketing and CRM to dispatch, invoicing, and payments, it holds a closed-loop dataset that competitors cannot replicate unless they have the same access.

So far, “Atlas” has recently executed its first fully automated job from booking to dispatch. For an industry constrained by labor shortages, this is a major development. So, the way I view this is that “Atlas” can become an effective way to improve efficiency and drive up ROI for customers. As customers begin to see these results flow through to the bottom line, I believe adoption of higher-value products will accelerate.

Valuation

Management guided for FY26 revenue of $935 million to $940 million, implying ~22% growth vs. FY25. My forward growth estimates are that TTAN will grow 20% through FY28, and I really don’t think 20% is a high bar since “Atlas” should see significant adoption in the coming years, and also NDRR is already at 110%, so TTAN just needs to grow 10% new logos to meet 20%, something that is not hard given the TAM is so large. In terms of multiples, I did not assume an upgrade here since I am assuming growth to stay at 20%, but should growth accelerate, multiples should go up, which will drive up the share price target.

Investment Risk

Operationally, TTAN relies heavily on third-party software and services for core features like payments and cloud hosting. Any disruption with these partners or a failure to maintain these relationships could adversely affect service quality and platform reliability, impacting customer satisfaction and the company's reputation. Another risk is the company's significant exposure to small and medium-sized businesses, which constitute a majority of its customer base. These customers can be more difficult and costly to retain than large enterprises and often have higher rates of business failure because SMBs are typically more exposed to the macro cycles.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for TTAN. There are solid structural growth drivers like enterprise adoption, high retention, PE consolidation tailwinds, and the potential of “Atlas.” This business should also easily grow given the large TAM and expanding platform stickiness. To me, the risks around SMB exposure and third-party dependencies are real but outweighed by the long runway and defensible data moat. Until proven otherwise, I see TTAN as an attractive stock to go long.

data.title: Huntington: Attractive As Veritex Deal Progresses
data.description:
Shares of Huntington Bancshares (NASDAQ:HBAN) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining about 21%. The regional bank has delivered strong credit underwriting results, while its efforts to push into growth markets like the Carolinas and Texas are supporting growth. Recently, the company received good regulatory news in its acquisition of Veritex (VBTX), which should further accelerate its growth trajectory. I last covered shares of Huntington in July, rating the stock a “Buy,” but since then it has returned just 4% vs. the market’s 8% gain. With recent developments, now is a good time to revisit HBAN.

On Friday Oct. 3, the Federal Reserve granted approval for HBAN to complete its acquisition of Veritex, noting that the Department of Justice review found no potential risk to competition. This approval is not surprising to me, as I expected the deal to close with limited regulatory risk. HBAN is well-managed, and the two banks have limited overlap. Even after this deal, HBAN is still just a mid-sized regional bank, so there really is no supervisory concern. Huntington guided to a Q4 2025 closing of this purchase, and is on track to do so. I expect the deal to close within the next few weeks.

Veritex will add $9 billion of loans and $11 billion of deposits to Huntington’s business. Veritex adds meaningful density to its operations in Dallas/Fort Worth and Houston, where it has 31 branches. Importantly, Veritex was a solid standalone institution with an 11% common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio. Pro forma for this acquisition, TX will become HBAN’s third-largest state with $15 billion of loans and $13 billion of deposits, accelerating HBAN’s growth profile given the state’s favorable demographics.

Importantly, Huntington’s growth initiatives are already generating results. Since mid-2024, it has grown its loan book by nearly 10%, with loan growth continuing so far in Q3, as disclosed in September. Loan growth has been difficult to achieve in this environment, given elevated macro uncertainty, challenges in commercial real estate (“CRE”), and high rates, which discourage borrowing. While core markets are growing, its net initiatives and growth regions, the Carolinas and Texas, account for a disproportionate 40% of loan growth, aggressively ramping up as HBAN takes share and builds scale. This success on a standalone basis increases my confidence in the ability of HBAN to take the Veritex franchise and accelerate growth across TX.

Huntington’s operating model should also ease the integration of Veritex. While still a regional bank, HBAN’s footprint is becoming increasingly national, as it expands outside its core Midwest base. However, it runs its business separately across 12 regions, each led by a local president. This ensures strong connectivity to the local community, deepening expertise and leading to better credit loss outcomes, a hallmark of HBAN’s recent performance. This quasi-decentralized approach should help maintain Veritex’s connectivity to markets inside of Texas while providing better economies of scale across a larger organization.

Importantly, Huntington has been performing well on a standalone basis ahead of this merger. In the company’s second quarter, Huntington earned $0.34 per share. It took a $58 million loss repositioning a portion of its $45 billion securities portfolio. Essentially, it sold bonds bought when rates were lower at a loss and then repurchased bonds with a higher interest rate given the current rate environment. It will recoup this loss via higher net interest income of about $20 million per year, leading to a ~3-year payback. This shift also improved its adjusted CET1 ratio by 6bps. HBAN’s net interest margin expanded 1bp sequentially to 3.11%; this was up 12bps from a year ago as low-rate fixed maturities continue to mature.

HBAN continues to show solid deposit growth as average deposits were up 6.4% from last year and 1.1% sequentially. Importantly, HBAN has been able to do this while passing on much of the Fed’s rate cuts. So far this cycle, it has had a 38% deposit rate beta. I like to see a beta in the 35%-45% range, so it is encouraging to see HBAN meet this threshold while still expanding its deposit base so solidly.

On the other side of its balance sheet, average loans were up 7.9%. HBAN generated $2.4 billion of sequential loan growth in Q2 even as it worked down CRE balances by $200 million. Loan yields rose 4bps sequentially to 5.91%, thanks to a favorable mix shift. Importantly, loan quality remains strong, as net charge-offs were only 20bps. I view levels below 25bps as indicative of strong underwriting. Even as HBAN grows its loan portfolio, it is maintaining underwriting discipline.

HBAN is also well reserved with $2.5 billion of allowances, providing 1.86% allowance coverage of loans. It has just 63bps of nonperforming assets, a solid level, and reserves are adequate to cover NPLs as well as some deterioration should the economy slow further. Importantly, Veritex has similar underwriting standards and loan quality, and I see no cultural challenges incorporating their bankers into HBAN’s conservative lending culture.

HBAN is also making good progress on the capital front. The company is targeting an adjusted CET1 ratio including AOCI of 9%-10%. Last quarter, it reached 9%, having grown steadily for two years. Its published CET1 is 10.5%. For regulatory purposes, HBAN does not have to fully include AOCI losses in its capital calculation, but they are relevant economically, which is why management targets an adjusted measure. It has added 40bps of capital over the past year, and I expect it to end the year closer to 9.2%.

Speaking at a conference in September, Huntington said there could be nominal share repurchases this year after the close of Veritex. However, in 2026, it expects a more “programmatic” and material pace of buybacks. HBAN pays a secure 3.6% dividend, and I see scope for $250-$400 million of buybacks next year, as I still expect HBAN to retain capital to support loan growth. Still, its total capital return yield should be over 5% in 2026.

Back in July, I saw HBAN earning $1.45-$1.55 this year with $1.60+ in 2026 earnings power as it incorporates Veritex into its business. Given its securities portfolio repositioning, I now see $1.45-$1.51 this year, assuming no net contribution from Veritex. Q4 is likely to be a messy quarter from a GAAP perspective given M&A integration expenses. I continue to see ~$1.60 in 2026 earnings power, given $0.00-$0.05 of Veritex benefits.

With most regional banks trading at 9-13x, I believe HBAN should trade towards the higher end given its enticing combination of underwriting quality and balance sheet growth. I still view ~12x forward earnings as fair, or about $19, providing ~10% upside plus its dividend. As such, I see HBAN as a “Buy” and a core regional bank holding.

data.title: Progressive: 'AI Insurance Shopping' May End Marketing Edge (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Last October, I published a neutral outlook on The Progressive Corporation (NYSE:PGR) in "Progressive: Skyrocketing Car Insurance Costs Increase Consumer Price Sensitivity." PGR is effectively unchanged since then, seeing its bull market shift into stagnation as expected. My core thesis was that car insurance premiums would not continue to rise faster than repair costs, creating both growth and margin pressures on Progressive. Although the company may be "high quality" compared to many other insurers, its high valuation left it with little room for such a slowdown.

Progressive's EPS has continued to rise in 2025, but the analyst consensus now looks to stagnation in 2026 and beyond at an EPS of $16 to $18. PGR is still biased toward beating quarterly earnings expectations, but less often and to a lower degree than in 2024. This corresponds to a slowdown across the insurance industry, with the iShares Insurance ETF (IAK) also flat YoY. To me, this signals growing competitive pressure that is starting to undo profit margin growth seen after 2022.

PGR's forward "P/E" now appears slightly low at 13.2X. Its yield is smaller at 2%, but its short interest is just 1.1%, implying a lack of bearish speculation on it. Indeed, most analysts' views, here and on Wall Street, tend to range from neutral to positive, not bearish. However, it is facing a wave of downgrades, with Wells Fargo and BMO cutting outlooks last month, both citing competitive pressure. Given this, I think it's an ideal time to reconsider PGR and its macroeconomic backdrop to determine its risk profile going into Q4.

Technology-Driven Profit Reversal Grows

Technological advantages seen in the Progressive model that led to its abnormal growth are being more widely adopted by its competitors. 2024 was an excellent year for the company. It was at the forefront of "AI" utilization in reducing operating overhead costs, underwriting was streamlined, and the adoption of "telemantics" (devices that measure individual drivers' risk behavior) improved. The company used those savings to aggressively increase advertising, leading to it gaining market share dominance at just under 17%.

Innovation temporarily boosts market power and thereby profit margins, but in the end, competitors will adopt the same practices. Customers will gravitate toward carriers with the lowest premiums (for value), renewing profit margin pressures. Critically, with premiums up ~60% since 2020, customer loyalty is disappearing, with recent surveys finding 30% of policyholders switching in the past five years. Another survey found that 92% of people who switched carriers saved money, pointing to the fact that customer loyalty is often detrimental.

Progressive benefited from this initially, having a technological edge that often allowed for lower premiums, but that is changing. Geico, owned by Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), now has a slight edge over Progressive on many metrics. More specifically, I think that the rise of "AI tools" like GPT or others will accelerate competition. Insurance companies currently spend a great deal to gain the top spots on Google search. Still, now, people can easily prompt these tools, describe their car insurance needs, and may be pointed to the best value option, without bias from paid search (which will tend to show high-profit carriers at the top, not the best value). At least, that is my own anecdotal experience, and I expect many others are probably taking this insurance shopping approach.

In my view, car insurance company profits, over the long run, depend on marketing and customer loyalty. If people had the means to figure out which carrier would give them the best value for money, insurance premiums would naturally fall through competition to the "zero profit condition." However, good marketing can attract loyal customers who will overlook higher costs. This should remain a factor, but potentially fade with more people avoiding ad-heavy platforms (cable TV, adblocker, etc.).

Good customer retention practices (customer service lines, benefits for maintaining coverage, etc.) may keep customers from switching. AI may make it cheaper for carriers to determine risk. However, it also makes it easier for customers to "shop for value" more objectively (than advertising), potentially creating negative margin pressures over the next decade.

Allstate is the best public company to compare to Progressive, though it tends to score worse than both PGR and Geico in terms of customer value. I expect that to change as Allstate copies the insurance technology adopted by PGR. Indeed, though both have higher margins today than <2023, the margin gap between Progressive and Allstate is seemingly closing:

PGR's competitive edge was likely at a peak after Q3 2024, when I last covered it. Though the margin spread is still elevated, I think a reversal trend is developing. We also see this confirmed in the trend of motor vehicle insurance consumer prices compared to motor vehicle maintenance and repair costs. In last year's article, I pointed out the asymmetric rise in premiums-to-repair as a tailwind that was ending. This is becoming confirmed as insurance prices stagnate while repair costs are surging again:

The lower ratio chart is a good "barometer" for trends in the car insurance industry's profitability. This is more akin to "gross margins" of insurance, not accounting for more volatile changes in operating costs, particularly marketing and customer acquisition. Since the 2000s, the long-term trend has been toward lower repair costs to insurance premiums (or asymmetric premium growth). We've seen a few notable reversal periods, such as ~2004 to 2008 and ~2018 to 2022, which are reflected in lower PGR operating margins. This year, we're seeing signs that such a reversal is occurring again:

Vehicle repair costs are rising very quickly today due to a shortage of auto technicians (aging workforce and fewer entrants) and the last decade of vehicles being significantly more complex to fix. I think this results in more vehicles being replaced in an accident than repaired.

The Bottom Line

The combination of higher vehicle prices/repair costs, mixed with increasing consumer price sensitivity (particularly as insurance becomes more of a budget issue), I expect we will see negative headwinds on premiums (compared to benefits). To a large degree, this is the same view I had on Progressive last year, but now in an overtly negative manner. Last year, it was clear that PGR's competitive edge had reached an extreme. Looking over the next three years, I expect Progressive may lose some of the customers it's recently acquired as its competitors lower premiums, benefiting from the same technological advantages.

In the long run, I think Progressive may have added downside risk as its marketing edge is lost. The company is the most aggressive advertiser in the car insurance market. However, "all else being equal," more ad spend means higher premiums and lower customer value. All else is not equal, given Progressive has both more ad spending and generally more competitive premiums, but again, I believe it's starting to lose that edge as Geico and Allstate catch up in innovations.

More speculatively, AI "chat" models development may overhaul the value of advertising over the coming years. Traditionally, people learn about insurance companies through TV ads, online ads (increasingly blocked), paid search, and insurance agents. All of those methods are going to benefit higher premium companies that have excess profits to invest in customer acquisition. However, with AI research tools, people now have (virtually) free "insurance agents" that have no kickbacks.

This factor is speculative, because I cannot find much information proving people are doing this, only know that I found this shopping method extremely effective for changing insurance (and banking, etc.). This is probably an early trend, but basically, I see "AI tools" as a solution to the "Asymmetric Information" problem that drives profits in insurance (and many financial services).

As a result of these various trends, I expect Progressive's operating margins will fall back below 10%, implying a net margin of around 6%, as it realigns to the competitive dynamic of the 2010s. In other words, I think the post-2018 rise in insurance margins is largely transitory, resulting from "telemantics and AI innovations" that lose value as they become the industry norm.

If true, this should push PGR's EPS down to around $8 to $9, easily making PGR overvalued at a forward "P/E" around 26-30X. I do not expect that to occur in 2026, but probably closer to 2028, sooner if a recession triggers even more consumer price sensitivity. Its EPS may also rise with the general inflationary trend in revenues, but I will also be surprised if PGR's market share increases, and think a decline is more likely. Thus, I believe a forward "P/E" of around 15-20X of that $8-$9 target is most reasonable, corresponding to a target price of $120 to $180.

Because my view is long term, I would not bet against PGR today. It can easily continue to rise in the short term as its most immediate revenue and income trends are still "strong." The stock may also look undervalued to some based on its higher immediate EPS expectations. Still, by next year, I think far more investors and analysts will have a bearish tone on insurance due to a rebound in competitive pricing pressure, a sustained increase in price-sensitivity, and improved "shopping" methods.

data.title: Now Is A Great Time To Sell NovaGold (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
There is a time and season for everything. Over the last several years, I have been explaining the upside potential for NovaGold Resources Inc. (NYSE:NG). In my last NG article posted in February here, I argued the stock was a Strong Buy for patient gold bugs and those looking for a leverage-to-gold hedge in portfolio design.

Since that effort, NG has spiked from $3 to over $10 per share, which was my summary forecast earlier this year (from the February article),

My opinion is NovaGold's investment value keeps increasing on rising gold quotes, and the price is now coiled like a spring ready for release. At some point, perhaps in the weeks ahead as we cross $3000 an ounce, large investment managers and retail investors will wake up to the bullish gold story. They may pile into the more speculative development names like NovaGold. The end result could be another NG price advance above $10 a share, just like what happened in 2020.

The catalyst for the sharp price advance has been the unstoppable jump in gold during 2025. Yet, another important kick in the pants came when Barrick Mining (B) unexpectedly sold its 50% stake in Donlin Gold to NovaGold (increasing its share from 50% to 60%) and Paulson Advisers (with 40% ownership in total today). NG paid $200 million to Barrick, while Paulson transferred $800 million in the June transaction.

This week management outlined an ambitious 2031 production start goal, with a new feasibility study to be completed in two years.

All good news for shareholders, no doubt. However, now that NovaGold's stock spike has played out, what (if anything) should investors do with their holdings? Since I prefer to capture oversized gains, I suggest investors lock-in profits and wait for lower prices to reenter a position. At the very least, please consider trimming your ownership size as a rational, risk-adjusted decision. Why? Two reasons.

Sell Reason #1

NovaGold is still a resource development company, with lots of work and risks ahead of it. The company will likely have to issue plenty of shares and/or sell material debt to get construction going, years down the road. Total mine development to build costs were estimated in the 2021 Technical Report at $7.4 billion to move the asset into production, with $1.7 billion in sustaining costs required. In today's inflation-adjusted dollars, the total capital needed to start production is all but guaranteed to be well above $10 billion.

My guess is NovaGold will have to raise $7-8 billion over the next five years to construct Donlin Creek as cost-inflation numbers continue to rise (which represents a 60% stake in the unique and amazing Donlin district resource in Alaska, with 39 million in identified gold ounces in the ground). Given today's $4.1 billion market cap, we could be talking about a triple in shares outstanding (assuming no debt is used), using $10 for a quote.

What is NovaGold worth? That's a great question, and a very important one to ask, as gold investor euphoria has exploded on the scene.

My back-of-the-envelope estimates for a steady bull rise in gold to $5,000 an ounce in two years and $6,000 in five provide a target range over time of $20 to $30 a share for NovaGold (which is quite a distance from $3 earlier this year). I am calculating a total operating expense (including massive CAPEX depreciation) of $1,200 (today) to $1,500/oz. (by 2031) for gold production, with annual rates of 1.1 million ounces extracted for the whole project.

NG's 60% share under these assumptions works out to around $2.5 billion in sales at the current gold price of $3,800, potentially rising to $4 billion at $6,000/oz. gold. Using a 30% long-term tax rate, earnings would be around $1.2 billion now, potentially rising to $2 billion in 5-6 years. I would call these numbers a best-case scenario at this stage.

If we use the similar low-cost production profile, clean balance sheet, and Tier-1 mine safety jurisdictions of peer gold miner Agnico Eagle (AEM), a valuation yardstick today of 8x sales and 20x earnings would be the equivalent of a total NovaGold market cap in the $20-24 billion range (if the mine was in operation during 2025 and hitting the latest 2021 Technical Report estimates).

Sounds great, but after the huge share dilution to get into production, the current market cap at $10 per share would already be closer to $12-13 billion. If things worked out perfectly for NovaGold, I am projecting $30 per share for patient and lucky investors in 5-6 years, using a $6,000 gold price.

However, if gold prices actually decline over the next 2-3 years, with $3,000 our final destination, it is theoretically possible management and Paulson Advisers decide to hold off on building Donlin Gold. Such would likely bring a drop in the share price back to a minimal speculative value, maybe all the way down to $3 or $4 again. That's the gamble you take in a resource asset under development.

Sell Reason #2

I have harped on trading/investing in gold (and silver and platinum) as a function of underlying long-term worth, measured over many decades. My proprietary model for gold that compares the current price to U.S. money supply increases and Treasury debt changes (future required money printing), peer precious metal quotes like silver/copper/platinum, land and farm values in America, plus the stock market's relative price zone, in aggregate, can highlight long-term holding worth for the world's premier hard money asset.

Today, my model suggests gold should be valued around US$3,300 an ounce, likely climbing to $3,400 by early 2026. When gold was going for less than $2,000/oz. a couple of years ago, the downside risk appeared limited (to me) holding precious metal assets. My target price design is a median number, where half of the time gold trades above my average calculation and half below (going back to the mid-1960s, with a high degree of success in real-time use for 25 years). Right now, I am forecasting something of a coin flip for gold's price direction over the next year or two.

It is entirely possible $4,000 and $5,000 gold will appear over time, especially if a Treasury bond confidence crisis leads to exceptional weakness in the U.S. dollar exchange rate. Yet, this view has quickly become conventional wisdom in many corners of Wall Street. Such makes me nervous that gold miners and resource assets like NG are likely discounting higher gold quotes already.

On the other hand, I would not be shocked by a sub-$3,000 gold price in 12 months, upending bullish forecasts by analysts. In other words, NovaGold does carry dramatically higher risk to own at $10 vs. $3 a share, as it is a leveraged proxy to changing gold quotes.

Final Thoughts

My rating conclusion is I would put a Hold rating on shares, if I knew the gold price would stay above $3,500 during 2026. The problem with this view is a stock market bubble in general in America, and the growing odds of recession soon (Wall Street firms are sitting at 30% to 90% odds of a recession right now, for a 12-month forecast) could be extra problematic for NovaGold.

Gold mining stocks do have a history of initial underperformance of the S&P 500 during major bear markets and recessions. If we witness both, on top of a drop in gold (even a minor one), NG could easily decline back to $7 or $6 a share, as investors rush to capture the 2025 share gain.

So, I feel a 12-month rating of Sell for NovaGold may be more realistic. In my opinion, the odds are much greater than 50/50 that NG will be priced below $10 per share by next autumn. I will say, if a rating choice existed between Hold and Sell, I would put that suggestion on NovaGold at $10 in October 2025.

I do think a share offering today around $10 would be a prudent course of action for management. Raising $500 million to $1 billion now would give the company plenty of money for the new feasibility study and funding extra drilling to upgrade the property resources. Plus, it might push off another capital raise until mine construction is well underway in 2028 (assuming a green-light decision is reached). The short-term effect on the share price would likely be to limit gains, a direct result of added supply.

Of course, if gold prices continue to march higher, NG will almost surely rise a little more. Just keep in mind, the high rate of investment return experienced over the past six months has a very low probability of being repeated over the next 6-12 months.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Ovid Therapeutics: OV329 Promising For Neuronal Excitability In Neurological Disorders
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Ovid Therapeutics Inc. (NASDAQ:OVID), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Ovid Therapeutics: Data Readouts For Rare Epileptic Indications Late 2024." I mentioned the opportunity of the company advancing the drug soticlestat in the phase 3 SKYLINE and SKYWAY studies targeting patients with refractory Dravet Syndrome [DS] and Lennox-Gastaut Syndrome [LGS]. Unfortunately, both of these late-stage studies failed to hit the intended primary endpoint mark to continue on towards possible regulatory filings. At that time, I had a Buy rating on the basis of these two ongoing programs. I am revisiting this stock and placing a Hold rating for now, as the company has to prove that its new pipeline is capable of reaching late-stage clinical trial development.

With that being said, it is on the right track because just today it reported positive results from its phase 1 healthy volunteer study using its next-generation GABA-aminotransferase [GABA-AT] inhibitor OV329 to treat patients with drug-resistant epilepsies. It was shown that this drug was able to achieve a significant reduction of GABA-AT as initial proof of concept. The reason why this is good is because a decrease of GABA-AT means an increase of GABA and a decrease of neuronal excitability. This program is coming along well, as the goal is to initiate a phase 2a randomized, placebo-controlled study in Q2 of 2026 for the treatment of adults with drug-resistant focal onset seizures [FOS]. From there, clinical data from this mid-stage trial would be expected around mid-2027.

The second reason why I believe this stock remains a Hold rating is because the company has two candidates in its pipeline being developed to treat psyschoses involved with Parkinson's Disease [PD], Lewy Body Dementia [LBD], and Schizophrenia. This would be in terms of the development of OV4071 as an oral KCC2 Direct Activator and then an intravenous [IV] version candidate coined as OV350. In terms of OV350, it is currently being evaluated in a phase 1 healthy volunteer study with initial safety and pharmacokinetic [PK] data expected in Q4 of 2025, and this is one milestone to keep an eye on. Oral OV4071 is being studied to complete a package to be sent to regulators so that clearance can be achieved to initiate a phase 1/1b single-ascending dose [SAD]/Multiple-ascending dose [MAD] study. Regulatory clearance filing and subsequent trial initiation of this trial are expected in Q1 and Q2 of 2026, respectively.

OV329 Achieves Initial Proof Of Mechanism Of Action With Significant Inhibition of GABA-AT

As I noted above, the company was able to release initial positive biomarker findings from its phase 1 healthy volunteer study for the targeting of patients with drug-resistant epilepsy. The reason why this program is highly promising is because it is looking to improve upon existing GABA-aminotransferase [GABA-AT] inhibition. That is, this company holds the potential to have the best-in-class GABA-AT inhibitor. The ultimate end-game goal of this candidate is to be developed to treat drug-resistant epilepsy. Before going over the newly released phase 1 healthy volunteer study data, plus any catalysts that are to come about from this program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being for this company.

Epilepsy is characterized as a neurological disorder with recurrent seizures followed by subsequent periods of normal brain activity. This is a devastating disorder because the type of symptoms in patients who experience seizures can vary. That is, each person is different in how they respond to them. For example, one patient could have a blank stare into space for a few seconds, and then another might experience it through twitching of the arms or legs. The most important classification of having epilepsy is to have two unprovoked seizures at least 24 hours apart. This disorder can be further broken down into the drug-resistant category. This occurs in patients with epilepsy who fail to respond to multiple antiepileptic drugs [AEDs]. That is, seizure control is unattainable using several of these drugs. The bottom line despite medications being readily available for these patients, they don't end up working in a large segment of them.

The global epilepsy drugs market size is projected to reach $15.4 billion by 2030. This is a very large market opportunity, but the goal of this company is to specifically focus on patients who have drug-resistant focal onset seizures [FOS]. If the company sticks to solely focusing only on the targeting of this epileptic seizure patient population, then it could go after roughly 30% of the entire epilepsy market. This is where a next-generation GABA-AT like OV329 may come into play in that it has the mechanism of action [MOA] of inhibiting or inactivating GABA-AT and, in tandem, increasing GABA levels.

Why is this necessary? It is because the higher the GABA levels in a patient, the lower the neuronal excitability that is present. The rationale for doing this is because in preclinical testing this drug achieved the ability to reduce both focal and generalized seizures. Including pharmaco-resistant seizure types. The main thing to note is that this drug has an excellent risk-benefit profile because of the ability for low and repeat dosing. This is said to achieve a greater benefit of increasing GABA levels in the brain. The ability to achieve such dosing is thanks to it having a short half-life and also being eliminated from tissue in a rapid manner.

Think of it this way: there exists an FDA-approved GABA-AT inhibitor drug known as Vigabatrin [VGB] that is approved to treat infantile spasms and refractory complex partial seizures. The thing is that it works well in treating seizures but is plagued by one major issue, and that is accumulation of drug in the retina of the eye. It is not clear the long-term accumulation of this problem in the eye, but one reason is because VGB has a racemic mixture of both enantiomers (S)-(+) and (R)-(-). On the other hand, OV329 is only synthesized using the (S+) isomer. Thus, this next-generation version could end up being highly competitive with existing GABA-AT inhibitors like VGB.

The phase 1 trial tested oral capsules of OV329 in a total of 68 healthy volunteers. It is important to note that this is further broken down into a total of 51 patients that took drug and then 17 patients that were given a placebo instead. However, the data that was released focused on biomarker analyses of only patients in the following MAD cohorts:

  • 6 patients 3 mg oral OV329 drug versus 2 on placebo
  • 15 patients 5 mg oral OV329 drug versus 5 on placebo.

The final data showed that this next-generation version of drug was able to achieve statistically significant inhibition of GABA-AT. Again, this is very good because the lower the levels of this enzyme, the greater the GABA levels in place to reduce neuronal excitability. Remember above? Where I mentioned VGB being used to treat seizures? Well, in the news release, it was shown that OV329 either matched or exceeded inhibition of GABA-AT. This would be with respect to 5 mg of OV329 achieving an increase of inhibition by 53%, versus placebo only achieving a 24.9% change. Remember, the goal is to reduce the enzyme GABA-AT as much as possible, and it is because if not, this enzyme left in place breaks down GABA levels in the body. In turn, lower levels of GABA mean a huge increase in neuronal excitability and increased seizure exposure.

With that being said, there are a few catalysts to keep an eye on with respect to the development of this particular GABA-AT inhibitor. The first of which is that the biotech is engaging with regulators to be able to initiate a phase 2a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study using OV329 to target patients with drug-resistant focal onset seizures in Q2 of 2026. Should this timeline be met, then the company expects to complete this trial in mid-2027, with data likely thereafter.

KCC2 Direct Activation Candidate Provides An Alternative Targeting of Neuronal Balance

There is actually another reason for placing this company's stock with a Hold rating, and this is because it has another drug in its pipeline looking to establish neuronal balance. This would be with two drugs in the pipeline that are potassium-chloride cotransporter 2 (KCC2) direct activators as follows:

  • OV350 as an intravenous version of this drug candidate
  • OV4071, an oral version of this drug candidate.

This chloride transporter found in the central nervous system [CNS] is responsible for both controlling neuronal excitability presence and also the levels of GABA in place to treat patients with neurological conditions. Intravenous OV350 is set out to be explored in a healthy volunteer study to see if it can establish an effect in a patient's brain and be tested later on for a variety of neurological indications. Speaking of this trial in humans, it is expected that safety, PK, and tolerability data from this evaluation are to be released in Q4 of 2025. The purpose of the other study, using oral KCC2 direct activation OV4071, is to be used to place a focus on treating psychoses in patients with PD, LBD, and Schizophrenia.

There is a goal to file for regulatory clearance of this oral KCC2 activator candidate in Q1 of 2026 and then, from there, be in a position to initiate a phase 1/1b SAD/MAD study in Q2 of 2026 upon receiving clearance. The last item to mention is that these candidates are the first of many, as the company's proprietary library houses additional KCC2 activators to bring forth into clinical development.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Ovid Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $38.3 million as of June 30, 2025. The company estimated that it believed this would be enough cash on hand to fund its operations into the early part of the 2nd half of 2026. However, instead of waiting to raise cash later on, it just enacted a private placement agreement to raise approximately $175 million in total gross proceeds. It is important to note, though, that this is going to be split up. Upon closing of this transaction, the company is set to obtain $81 million, and this is crucial because it is said to be enough to fund its operations into 2028. There is another $94 million in total gross proceeds possible with the exercise of issued warrants.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Ovid Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the development of its next-generation GABA-AT inhibitor, OV329. The reason why is because, as I have shown above, it was able to achieve the mode of action of inhibition of this enzyme in a statistically significant manner in a phase 1 healthy volunteer study. The next phase of testing is to explore this drug in the targeting of adults with focal onset seizures. The risk here is that even though initial MOA was established with OV329, there is no assurance that it will be shown yet again in this expected phase 2a study against this epilepsy patient population.

The second risk to consider would be with respect to its intravenous [IV] KCC2 activator OV350. That's because it is the first time that this MOA is being explored in humans in a healthy volunteer study, and there is no way of knowing if the drug will be tolerable or safe to take for starters. Nor that the initial pharmacokinetic profile is going to be ideal to move this drug candidate forward to additional clinical testing.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the oral version of its KCC2 activator drug it is working on, known as OV4071. The goal is to initiate a proof-of-concept study for this program in early 2026. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that the final readout of this trial is going to be positive. Even if data from this initial proof-of-concept trial is positive, the next stage is to evaluate the use of oral KCC2 activator OV4071 for the treatment of psychoses found in PD, LBD, and Schizophrenia patients. The risk with this is that there is no assurance that this drug is going to effectively treat this symptom found in these patients.

Conclusion

Ovid Therapeutics did have to cut out its development of soticlestat for the treatment of patients with DS and LGS. Despite this, it has been able to bring to the forefront two drug candidates that are to deal with neuronal excitability with certain neurological disorders. OV329 takes care of neuronal excitability in terms of reducing GABA-AT enzymes and in turn increasing GABA activity found in patients. On the other hand, KCC2 activation involves activating GABA but also regulating neuronal balance by restoring chloride homeostasis in neurons.

The positive results shown today from the phase 1 healthy volunteer study, at the very least, establish the mode of action of next-generation OV329, which is the inhibition of GABA-AT enzymes. The hope is that it is going to be able to successfully clear the next expected trial, which would be a phase 2a study using this drug candidate to treat adults with drug-resistant focal onset seizures. With positive results released from its next-generation GABA-AT inhibitor OV329 in a healthy volunteer study, plus the expected first-ever safety and pharmacokinetic data from an early-stage study of IV OV350 expected in Q4 of 2025, I believe that this stock should remain with a Hold rating for the time being.

data.title: FNDF: Benchmark-Beating Ex-U.S. Value ETF With Dividend Growth
data.description:
This article updates my review of Schwab Fundamental International Large Company Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FNDF) published in April in light of recent performance and current holdings.

FNDF strategy

FNDF was launched on 08/15/2013 and tracks the RAFI Fundamental High Liquidity Developed ex US Large Index. It was tracking the Russell RAFI Developed ex US Large Company Index prior to 6/21/2024. The change in underlying indexes had a very minor impact on the strategy, as both use the RAFI methodology in similar stock universes. FNDF has a portfolio of 925 stocks, a net expense ratio of 0.25%, and a 30-day SEC yield of 2.81%. Distributions are paid semi-annually.

As described by Schwab, the underlying index starts from the RAFI Global Equity Investable Universe and limits its scope to non-US developed markets. Companies are selected and weighted using a fundamental size score based on sales, operating cash flow, dividends, and buybacks. The top 87.5% of the companies by cumulative size score are included in the index. The index is partially reconstituted every quarter in four equal segments, resulting in an annual reconstitution. The portfolio turnover rate was 12% in the most recent fiscal year and 11% in the previous year. I will use as a benchmark iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA), which tracks a developed markets equity index excluding the U.S. and Canada.

Portfolio

FNDF is mostly invested in large and mega caps, with 78% of asset value in companies over $15 billion of market capitalization. Europe is the heaviest region, with about 49% of assets. The top two countries are Japan (24.1%) and the U.K. (14.8%). Other countries weigh no more than 9%. Compared to EFA, FNDF slightly overweights Japan, downplays Switzerland, and includes Canada and South Korea.

The fund has significant exposure in financials (19%) and industrials (16.5%). Compared to the benchmark, FNDF overweights mostly materials and energy, while it significantly downplays financials, healthcare, and real estate.

The portfolio has low company risk. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent only 12.9% of assets, and the heaviest position weighs 2.7%.

Performance

FNDF has outperformed EFA by 81 bps in annualized return since 8/22/2013, with a higher risk measured by maximum drawdown and standard deviation of monthly returns. Risk adjusted performance (measured by the Sharpe ratio in the table below) stays superior to the benchmark, though.

The annual sum of distributions has increased by 183% in 10 years, from $0.47 per share in 2014 to $1.33 in 2024. This dividend growth rate has nicely outpaced inflation (about 34% in the same time, based on CPI). However, it wasn’t a steady uptrend: distributions decreased in 2020 and 2022.

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics of FNDF and five ETFs in the Foreign Large Value category:

  • Dimensional International Value ETF (DFIV)
  • iShares MSCI EAFE Value ETF (EFV)
  • Invesco RAFI Developed Markets Ex-US ETF (PXF)
  • Avantis International Large Cap Value ETF (AVIV)
  • Alpha Architect International Quantitative Value ETF (IVAL)

FNDF has the lowest expense ratio and turnover. It is in second position in size (assets under management) and liquidity (dollar volume) behind EFV. Its performance since October 2021 is average compared to peers, with DFIV and EFV outperforming it significantly in total return and Sharpe ratio.

Takeaway

Schwab Fundamental International Large Company Index ETF (FNDF) has a portfolio of large-cap ex-US companies selected and weighted with the RAFI methodology based on fundamental size. FNDF has a focus on Europe and Japan, with strong value characteristics, broad diversification across holdings and sectors, and cheap fees. FNDF has outperformed an ex-US developed markets benchmark since its inception, but underperformed DFIV and EFV over the last few years.
